fuzzy_prd:
hey team, so we got some complaints from the frontend folks that CORS is still being a pain in certain edge cases. basically we need to build out that cross-origin middleware thing we talked about last sprint. the core idea is: devs should be able to define reusable policies and attach them to URL patterns, and the middleware should just handle all the preflight/response header stuff automatically without them needing to touch every endpoint.

the tricky parts i keep hearing about are: (1) sometimes the browser sends a method name in a weird casing and the current approach just rejects it silently, which is super confusing — we need to handle that gracefully somehow, similar to how we handled the case-sensitivity stuff in the auth header module last quarter; (2) there's also this ask from the platform team to support a 'hard override' for the allow-origin header value for certain deployments where they can't use the reflected origin pattern; (3) policy selection needs to be smart — different URL areas should get different rules, and some rules should only kick in for specific hostnames, not all traffic; (4) when multiple policy sources are active at the same time, later ones should win on a field-by-field basis, not replace the whole thing. priority ordering should also be configurable. we don't want to break anything that's already working for same-origin traffic. someone check how the existing request phase handling works before touching that part.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the client sends an Access-Control-Request-Method header with a value that has the same letters but different casing than a configured allowed method (e.g., client sends 'Link' but config has 'LINK'), the response must include both the originally configured method form AND the client-sent form appended to the Access-Control-Allow-Methods header. For example, if configured methods are ['LINK', 'PUT'] and the client requests 'Link', the header value must be 'LINK, PUT, Link'.

The policy supports an 'override_allow_origin' field. When set, the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header must use this override value regardless of the incoming Origin header. This applies in all scenarios: successful preflight, rejected preflight, accepted actual requests, and requests without an Origin header. The override value is emitted verbatim — it can be '*' or a space-separated list of origins like 'http://example.com http://other.com'.

trigger_keywords: preflight response vs actual response headers
which headers appear on OPTIONS vs regular requests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For actual (non-preflight) cross-origin requests processed in the response phase, the output must NOT include preflight-only headers with values. Specifically, Access-Control-Allow-Methods and Access-Control-Allow-Headers must be empty in the response for actual requests. Only Access-Control-Allow-Origin (and credentials/expose headers if configured) should be populated. The Vary header should also be empty for actual requests (no 'Origin' entry).

trigger_keywords: request from same host not blocked
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the request's Origin header matches the request host (same-origin), the request phase must output 'response_created=no' and must not generate any synthetic HTTP response. Similarly, if the origin does not match any allowed origin in the policy and the request is not a preflight OPTIONS request, the request phase must also output 'response_created=no'. In both cases, the output only includes phase, url, and method lines.

Path-based policy selection iterates the configured path_policies in insertion order and returns the FIRST pattern that matches the request path. Earlier-listed patterns take precedence over later ones. If no pattern matches, the default policy values are returned. Pattern matching is regex-based (e.g., '^/test/regex' will match before '^/test/' if listed first and the path matches both).

trigger_keywords: host pattern restriction on path policy
skip policy if host does not match
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A path policy can include a 'host_patterns' list of regex strings. When host_patterns is set, that path policy is only eligible if the request host matches at least one of the host patterns. If the host does not match, that path policy is skipped and matching continues to the next path policy entry. A path policy without host_patterns applies to all hosts. The output format for host_patterns in configuration_match is a comma-space separated list of the raw pattern strings.

When multiple policy providers are merged (provider_merge interaction), later providers override earlier providers on a field-by-field basis. If a later provider omits a key, the earlier provider's value for that key is preserved. For example, if provider 1 sets exposed_headers=['c','d'] and provider 2 does not include exposed_headers, the merged result still has exposed_headers='c, d'. Only keys explicitly present in the later provider's policy replace the earlier value.

In the provider_order interaction, providers are sorted in ascending numeric priority order. Providers without an explicit priority value are treated as the lowest priority (path_configuration built-in provider) and appear first in the ordered list. Explicitly prioritized providers follow in ascending order of their priority number. Providers sharing the same priority number maintain their original relative order. The built-in path_configuration provider always appears as provider_0 in the output.

A path policy can enable 'origin_pattern_matching: true', which causes the allowed_origins list to be treated as regex patterns rather than literal origin strings. For example, '^http://(.*)\.example\.com' would match any subdomain of example.com. This is a distinct boolean flag from the allowed_origins list itself. When output in configuration_match, origin_pattern_matching is shown as 'yes' or 'no'.

When a policy sets allowed_headers to '*' (a wildcard string rather than a list), it means all request headers are allowed. In the configuration_match output, this is rendered as 'allowed_headers=*'. In a preflight response, when the client sends Access-Control-Request-Headers, the Access-Control-Allow-Headers response header should reflect the configured allowed_headers value (the literal list or wildcard as configured).

trigger_keywords: vary header actual vs preflight response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'vary=Origin' line must appear in the output for preflight responses (OPTIONS requests handled by the policy layer). For actual (non-preflight) cross-origin responses, vary must be empty (vary=). This applies even when override_allow_origin is set — if the exchange was a preflight OPTIONS request, vary=Origin is still included in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the case-sensitivity stuff in the auth header module last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The method case-mismatch logic in feature1_preflight_success.json: when Access-Control-Request-Method 'Link' differs in case from configured 'LINK', both forms must be appended to Access-Control-Allow-Methods — see the second test case in feature1_preflight_success.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: someone check how the existing request phase handling works before touching that part
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The cors_exchange interaction with exchange=request in feature4_non_cors_and_unmatched_origin.json — same-origin and unmatched-origin requests must pass through with response_created=no, no synthetic HTTP response is generated

context_pointers.vague_reference: multiple policy sources are active at the same time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The provider_merge interaction defined in feature7_provider_merge.json and the provider_order interaction in feature8_provider_priority_order.json — these define how multiple PolicyProvider instances are composed and ordered

context_pointers.vague_reference: hard override for the allow-origin header value for certain deployments
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The override_allow_origin field in the policy object, covered by feature3_forced_origin_override.json — when set, it replaces the reflected origin in Access-Control-Allow-Origin for all request types

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from the business side. You'd need to look at the existing test cases and the middleware behavior documentation to determine the exact expected output. Check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory for the relevant scenario and work from the observable input/output contracts there.

feature1_preflight_success.json.description: Preflight requests that match the configured cross-origin policy return an immediate HTTP response with the allowed origin, allowed methods, allowed request headers, and Origin in Vary.

cases.expected_output:
phase=request
url=/foo
method=OPTIONS
response_created=yes
status=200
header:Access-Control-Allow-Origin=http://example.com
header:Access-Control-Allow-Methods=POST, PUT
header:Access-Control-Allow-Headers=foo, bar
header:Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=
header:Access-Control-Expose-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Max-Age=
vary=Origin
body=

phase=request
url=/foo
method=OPTIONS
response_created=yes
status=200
header:Access-Control-Allow-Origin=http://example.com
header:Access-Control-Allow-Methods=LINK, PUT, Link
header:Access-Control-Allow-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=
header:Access-Control-Expose-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Max-Age=
vary=Origin
body=

feature2_actual_response_headers.json.description: Actual cross-origin requests that pass the policy do not receive a request-phase response, then the final HTTP response receives only actual-response CORS headers, not preflight-only headers.

cases.expected_output:
phase=response
url=/foo
method=POST
response_created=yes
status=200
header:Access-Control-Allow-Origin=http://example.com
header:Access-Control-Allow-Methods=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=
header:Access-Control-Expose-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Max-Age=
vary=
body=

feature3_forced_origin_override.json.description: When an override allow-origin value is configured, the emitted Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header uses that override value for matching preflight requests, non-matching preflight requests, accepted actual requests, and requests without an Origin header.

cases.expected_output:
phase=response
url=/foo
method=OPTIONS
response_created=yes
status=200
header:Access-Control-Allow-Origin=*
header:Access-Control-Allow-Methods=GET
header:Access-Control-Allow-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=
header:Access-Control-Expose-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Max-Age=
vary=Origin
body=

phase=response
url=/foo
method=GET
response_created=yes
status=200
header:Access-Control-Allow-Origin=http://example.com http://huh-lala.foobar
header:Access-Control-Allow-Methods=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Allow-Credentials=
header:Access-Control-Expose-Headers=
header:Access-Control-Max-Age=
vary=
body=

feature4_non_cors_and_unmatched_origin.j.description: Requests that are same-origin or whose Origin is not allowed by the policy continue without an immediate HTTP response during request processing.

feature5_path_policy_selection.json.description: Path-based policy selection returns the first configured path pattern that matches the request URL, falls back to defaults when no path matches, and can select regex-origin policies.

cases.expected_output:
allowed_origins=http://one.example.com
credentials=no
allowed_headers=
allowed_methods=GET
exposed_headers=
cache_seconds=0
host_patterns=
origin_pattern_matching=no
override_allow_origin=

allowed_origins=http://two.example.com
credentials=yes
allowed_headers=*
allowed_methods=PUT, POST
exposed_headers=X-CorsTest
cache_seconds=120
host_patterns=
origin_pattern_matching=no
override_allow_origin=

feature6_host_restricted_policy_selectio.description: A path policy with host restrictions is used only when the request host matches one of its host patterns; otherwise matching continues to the next eligible path policy.

cases.expected_output:
allowed_origins=http://domainmatch.example.com
credentials=yes
allowed_headers=*
allowed_methods=PUT, POST
exposed_headers=
cache_seconds=160
host_patterns=^test\., \.example\.org$
origin_pattern_matching=no
override_allow_origin=

allowed_origins=http://two.example.com
credentials=yes
allowed_headers=*
allowed_methods=PUT, POST
exposed_headers=X-CorsTest
cache_seconds=120
host_patterns=
origin_pattern_matching=no
override_allow_origin=

feature7_provider_merge.json.description: When multiple policy providers apply to a request, later providers override earlier providers key by key; omitted keys from later providers keep the earlier value.

cases.expected_output:
allowed_origins=c
credentials=no
allowed_headers=e
allowed_methods=
exposed_headers=c, d
cache_seconds=0
host_patterns=
origin_pattern_matching=no
override_allow_origin=x

feature8_provider_priority_order.json.description: Tagged policy providers are collected into the runtime resolver in ascending numeric priority order, with the built-in path-configuration provider included at the lowest priority.
